title: Chapter Fifty-Six: The Magistrate's Music
content:
## JEONG YEON-SHIN

The estate stank of wine and arrogance.

Yeon-shin stood in the wide courtyard of the Pingyin County magistrate's compound, Baek Mi-ryeo and Cheong Myeong at his flanks, and studied the scene before him. Lanterns strung between carved pillars. Women carrying trays. Concubines draped across silk cushions. And at the center of it all, a handsome man in official robes, holding a wine cup with such deliberate grace that it might have been a performance.

The golden *Hwang* character on Yeon-shin's shoulder was half-hidden by the angle of his body. The magistrate's eyes slid over him, over Mi-ryeo, and settled on Cheong Myeong.

"So there is one of the same clan," the magistrate said, smiling. "Surely you are not playing warrior with these children? I would advise against it."

Cheong Myeong said nothing. His face held an expression Yeon-shin had never seen on him before -- neither amused nor cold, but something between, like a man watching an insect crawl across his food.

"Are you the magistrate of Pingyin County?" Yeon-shin asked.

"Can you not tell by looking? I am quite well known in my own right."

The man's composure was remarkable. Even with armed strangers in his courtyard, he sat being served wine, his extended hand steady, his bearing that of a noble entirely at ease in his domain.

*They said it was a long-standing operation.* Desolate Sect had gathered intelligence before dispatching forces. Not only the Hwangbo main house but every branch family -- members, martial characteristics, local alliances. All mapped. All catalogued.

"I know the head of the Hwangbo branch in Pingyin County is your first wife," Yeon-shin said. "Call her out."

"How lacking in manners. Have you never studied Confucian learning?"

"A magistrate who colluded with martial arts families. What respect do you seek when your conduct is unworthy of a gentleman?"

At that, the woman massaging the magistrate's arm rose. She looked about thirty. Languid, heavy-lidded eyes. Red light armor under her robes, and an energy wave building around her like heat off sun-baked stone.

"I am Jin-lang's principal wife. If you are a Shandong martial artist, you must have heard of me." Her voice carried the certainty of a woman who had never been denied anything. "I am Hwangbo Mei-wei of the Deep Autumn Sword."

"As head of the Pingyin Hwangbo family, I command you to refrain from disrespectful words and actions. No -- it is already useless. What could have ended with an arm will now end with your head."

Warriors emerged from the halls. Over twenty. Though she belittled him in words, they did not underestimate the force that had destroyed the gate. Five of them advanced first -- solid, tempered, their qi carrying the weight of generations. Even a branch was rooted in one of the Eight Great Families.

Yeon-shin did not look at them. He stared at Deep Autumn Sword.

"Do you know Hwangbo Myeong-rin?"

"What?"

"Your talk of arms and heads. It is exactly the same."

"You seem to have heard something. She is an outstanding child, worthy of attending the Dragon Phoenix Gathering." A red smile formed on Mei-wei's lips, as if she had applied rouge. "Did you think we would be lenient if you spoke as if you knew our direct bloodline? Impossible."

*What a degenerate age.*

He recalled words that sect elders might say. And with them, the image of Desolate Sect's lord -- she who had reached the absolute realm, beautiful through dignity alone, without other adornments. A martial artist should be beautiful through supreme martial arts.

"I saw the marketplace," Yeon-shin said. "It seems true that you privately mobilized citizens for labor. You have extended your martial world authority to affect people's lives." He paused. "Are you not ashamed?"

Behind him, Cheong Myeong and Mi-ryeo stepped back. The blue-ranked youngest was to be given murim experience. They had discussed it -- no intervention unless the situation became truly dangerous.

Everyone in the estate laughed.

The magistrate and Deep Autumn Sword laughed loudest. Mouths wide. No hands raised to cover them.

"Child," Mei-wei said, her face bright with amusement. "You have heard many murim rumors. Power is simply power. Distinguishing between the murim and people's lives? How can anyone do that?"

"Martial arts families are simply noble families," the magistrate added. "How could it be wrong to enjoy luxuries befitting our history?"

"A noble denying imperial law," Yeon-shin said. "Your first wife's head will fly today."

More laughter. Even the advancing warriors joined in.

"You do not understand elegance," the magistrate said, and clapped his hands.

Musicians appeared. Zithers. Jade flutes. They took their positions with the practiced haste of servants who had done this many times before. Music began at the magistrate's gesture -- flowing, refined, genuinely beautiful.

Simultaneously, the warriors drew their swords. They approached with footwork that matched the rhythm, as if even swordplay was entertainment here.

*Incredible.*

The realization hit like a fist to the gut. Yeon-shin recalled stories from Desolate Sect intelligence -- tales of high officials who had tasted every pleasure, and found the ultimate entertainment was watching men die.

The scene before him was exactly that.

This was not something that had started yesterday.

"A young swordsman who can destroy the estate's gate," Mei-wei said, settling back into her seat, sharing wine cups with the magistrate. "Quite a regrettable talent."

"Would it not provide suitable entertainment?"

Shandong Province was vast. Jinan was its center. There must have been many local martial artists who had come here, filled with righteous indignation after seeing the citizens' forced labor.

They had killed them all. The music now flowing past his ears -- the warriors approaching with footwork like dancers. All of it rehearsed. All of it practiced on the bodies of heroes.

"Can't stand it," Yeon-shin muttered.

He recalled the sound of grass brushing that had welcomed their group when they first entered the village. Pleasant. Modest. This place was different.

Suffocating.

"You spoke of elegance," he said. He looked once around at the warriors who had formed a closing circle. Over fifty now, with those who had emerged from the halls at Mei-wei's snap of her fingers. "I too have recently practiced a melody."

The Northern Light sword vibrated in its scabbard. A resonance building from within, rising through the hilt into his palm. True qi surging through predetermined pathways, loading itself onto the blade's voice.

He glanced at Mi-ryeo and Cheong Myeong. They nodded, barely perceptible.

He stamped his foot. True angle. Concentric winds blew in all directions, and his internal energy -- deeper than it had been even days ago, refined through constant operation of Moon Spirit Harmony Technique -- erupted.

Northern Light sang.

Not a tone. Not a note. A *cry* -- long, piercing, rising from the blade like a living thing, and with it invisible waves of true qi surged outward in every direction, passing through flesh and bone and protective energy alike.

"Heup--"

"Ugh...?"

Short groans. Dozens of them, from every direction at once. Then dull sounds, everywhere -- bodies hitting stone, bodies hitting earth, bodies folding and crumpling and falling where they stood.

The sword song faded.

Its aftermath did not.

The encirclement had collapsed entirely. Hwangbo branch warriors lay in every direction, sprawled across the courtyard in the postures of the suddenly dead. Eyes wide open. Staring at nothing.

The music stopped.

Only a very few remained standing -- the magistrate, Deep Autumn Sword, and a handful of warriors who had been strong enough to survive.

"Evil sect trash talking about elegance." Yeon-shin looked around the courtyard of corpses, then sheathed Northern Light. "Seems it was quite moving for you."

---
